voice: naturalist, third-person (the animal is observed, not the speaker)
rules: Keep the 8 locked questions as the structural spine of any answer set.
Answers may use the full master bullet list and scientific vocabulary.
Be explicit when something is broadly accepted vs. still debated.
You may extend beyond the 8 questions only when a user asks a follow-up.
Cite scientific name, habitat, and conservation status when discussing the species.
Never pretend to be the animal — third-person, naturalist voice only.
where_lives: South America — wetlands, rivers, and flooded grasslands
locked_questions: What can it see, hear, or feel?
Where does it live — and why?
What does it do when something changes?

What can it see, hear, or feel?: Its eyes are set high on its head to spot danger from above.
It hears soft splashing sounds and barks from group members.
Its nose picks up the scent of each animal in its group.
It feels vibrations in the water when something moves nearby.
Where does it live — and why?: It lives near rivers, ponds, and flooded grasslands.
Water lets it hide quickly when a predator comes close.
It grazes on grasses that grow along the water's edge.
Mud baths keep it cool and protect its skin from the sun.
How does it send messages?: It barks sharply to warn the group of a nearby threat.
Young ones hum and purr almost constantly to stay in contact.
Males rub a scent gland on their snout onto plants to mark territory.
A low whistle from a female signals that she is ready to mate.
What does it do when something changes?: When startled, the whole group rushes into the nearest water.
It can hold its breath underwater for several minutes.
After disturbance, it becomes more active at night and more cautious during the day.
It stands still and alert before deciding whether to flee.
How does it learn?: Young capybaras learn to graze by following adults to feeding spots.
They stay near the group and watch adults for cues about danger.
Babies hear alarm calls and quickly learn which sounds mean run.
How does it have babies?: A female gives birth to two to eight young at one time.
Babies can walk, swim, and eat grass within their first week.
Several females in a group share nursing duties for all the young.
Young stay close to adults for protection from eagles and caimans.
What are its limits?: It must stay close to water, or it cannot escape large predators.
High heat slows it down; it rests in water or mud during hot hours.
Loud sudden sounds trigger the whole group to stop or scatter.
It cannot survive long in areas where water dries up completely.
What does this teach us?: One animal watching the edge of the water protects the whole group.
A habitat that looks safe from above may hide danger below.
What keeps it calm — shade, water, quiet — tells you what it needs.

closing_prompt: LOOK AT THE ANIMAL: What do you see it doing right now?
footer: What a living thing can sense becomes its reality.

alpha_omega_failure_cycle.description: Four-part per-species failure cycle. The Tier 2 LLM walks through these lists before answering a hard question about this species: what is solid, what is contested, how people commonly get it wrong, and what this species' hard rules are. Content is species-specific and derived from master bullets.
broadly_accepted: Fully semi-aquatic; water is the primary escape route from jaguars, pumas, and caiman.
Lives in tightly social groups with shared sentinel behavior.
Young can swim within the first week of life.
Several females in a group may nurse each other's young.
still_debated: How formal the 'dominance' hierarchy is outside breeding season.
Whether humming and purring carry specific information or just contact-keeping.
How group size adjusts to local predator density over years.
common_failure_modes: Over-stating how much it tolerates terrestrial habitat without water access.
Treating its calm temperament in viral video clips as the default wild state.
Confusing 'common in the wild' with 'unaffected by habitat loss.'
species_specific_guardrails: Do not imply it is a pet or low-maintenance animal.
Never describe it away from water as a long-term viable state.
Do not compare its group behavior to domestic livestock.
